source: Inference Engineering by Philip Kiely (Baseten Books, 2026, 259 pages, ISBN 979-8-9943597-2-3)
note: Curated facts the AI Mentor checks before making numeric/operational claims. Anti-hallucination layer. Each fact has a verifiable source (book chapter or official doc).

facts.claim: H100 ops:byte ratio in FP16 is approximately 295
H100 dense FP8 compute is 1979 TFLOPS
H100 has 80 GB HBM3 with 3.35 TB/s memory bandwidth
H200 has 141 GB HBM3e with 4.8 TB/s memory bandwidth (same compute as H100)
B200 is Blackwell with 192 GB HBM3e, ~5 petaFLOPS dense FP8, up to 8 TB/s memory bandwidth
B300 is Blackwell with 288 GB HBM3e (more memory than B200, same compute)
NVLink on Blackwell delivers 1800 GB/s GPU-to-GPU 1:1 bandwidth
NVLink on Hopper delivers 900 GB/s GPU-to-GPU 1:1 bandwidth
InfiniBand maxes at 400 Gb/s per NIC for inter-node communication
Ethernet maxes at 100 Gb/s per NIC, much slower than NVLink and InfiniBand
H100 supports MIG with up to 8 memory slices and 7 compute slices (132 SMs total)
DeepSeek V3.1 has 671B total parameters with 37B active per token (MoE)
Qwen3-235B-A22B is MoE with 235B total params, 22B active, 128 experts, router picks 8 per token in 94 layers
Whisper 3 Large has 1.55B parameters
Orpheus TTS is derived from Llama 3.2 3B

facts.validValues: integer >= 1, must divide model heads evenly
HuggingFace model ID, smaller model from same family as target
facts.context: Used with mlx_lm.server or mlx_lm.generate to enable speculative decoding

facts.claim: Speculative decoding improves TPS/ITL but NOT TTFT — it is a decode-phase optimization
LLM decode is memory-bandwidth-bound — model weights are loaded from HBM for every token generated
LLM prefill is compute-bound — input sequence is processed in parallel, weights loaded once
Image and video generation are compute-bound (not memory-bandwidth-bound like LLM decode), because they iterate over latent space in parallel each denoising step
KV cache transforms attention from O(N²) to O(N) per token during decode by reusing previously computed keys and values
Arithmetic intensity of attention during decode (N=4096, d=128, FP16) is ~62 ops/byte, far below H100 ops:byte 295 — confirming memory-bound
Few-step image generation (8 steps vs 30-50) achieves 80-90% speedup with notable but acceptable quality drop

In image generation, disabling classifier-free guidance for the last ~20 of 50 steps cuts forward passes from 100 to 80 with minimal quality loss — early steps establish outline, late steps refine details
Cold start for an inference replica is the sum of 4 additive factors: GPU procurement, container image loading, model weights loading, engine startup (compilation if applicable)
vLLM has roughly 2x the GitHub stars of SGLang and TensorRT-LLM combined as of book publication (Jan 2026)
Inference engine tradeoff: vLLM has best model/hardware support, SGLang excels at MoE multi-node throughput, TensorRT-LLM has best peak performance but hardest learning curve
Tensor Parallelism is generally best for low-latency inference within a single node; Expert Parallelism better for throughput in MoE; Pipeline Parallelism only for multi-node scenarios
Disaggregation (separating prefill and decode engines) is worth the complexity only with: high traffic (~100M-1B tokens/day), large model (>= 100B params), and prefill-heavy workload
Quantizing from FP16 to FP8 typically yields 30-50% performance improvement, NOT a clean 2x — overhead from scale factor handling consumes part of the theoretical 2x FLOPS
MXFP8 microscaling format uses block size 32 — one scale factor per 32 parameters
NVFP4 (NVIDIA proprietary 4-bit) uses block size 16 plus a secondary 32-bit global scale factor
KV cache storage hierarchy by bandwidth: G1 GPU VRAM (TB/s), G2 CPU RAM (10-100 GB/s), G3 local SSD (5-10 GB/s), G4 networked SSD (GB/s)
A single high-resolution image adds approximately 1000 visual tokens to a VLM input sequence
A 4-second HD video clip at 24fps tokenizes to roughly 100k tokens — downsampling resolution and frame rate is practically mandatory for VLM video processing
Optimized Whisper deployments achieve Real-Time Factor of ~1000X for long files (1 hour transcribed in <4 seconds), parallelized across MIGs or GPUs
TTS has a useful TPS ceiling of 80-100 tokens/sec — beyond that audio is generated faster than realtime, so optimizations should pivot to concurrent users not faster generation
In video generation models, attention accounts for 70-80% of compute time, making it the primary optimization target
Network latency adds approximately 5ms per US timezone of distance between user and server — significant for tight SLA budgets
From the Llama 3 paper: 16,000 H100 GPUs over 54 days experienced 419 unexpected interruptions — roughly 1 hardware failure per 50,000 GPU-hours
Apple Silicon (M-series) does NOT run CUDA — vLLM, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM are NVIDIA-only. Apple alternatives: MLX, MLX-LM, llama.cpp, Ollama
Apple M3 Ultra has 512 GB unified memory and 819 GB/s bandwidth (consumer prosumer tier)
NVIDIA RTX 5090 has 32 GB VRAM and 1792 GB/s bandwidth (consumer top tier)
